```
                                          (duplicated, no fixed interval)
                                                  |
                                              (with measured interval)
                                                  v
                                          [A . . A . . A . . A]
                                          (RHYTHM — perceived beat)
                                                  |
                                              (structured 2D tiling)
                                                  v
                                          [A A A]   [A A A]   [A A A]
                                          [A A A]   [A A A]   [A A A]
                                          (PATTERN — tessellated grid)
```

**Stage 1 — Repetition:**
- *Definition:* A visual element is duplicated one or more times.
- *Design example:* The **Apple logo** repeated identically on the back of every iPhone model.
- *Engineering / production benefit:* **Single source asset** — the same SVG/PNG file is reused, so storage and version-control overhead are minimized.

**Stage 2 — Rhythm:**
- *Definition:* Repetition with a *measured interval* creates a perceived cadence.
- *Design example:* **Equal-width column rules** in a magazine layout.
- *Engineering / production benefit:* **Predictable layout math** — designers can automate column placement using a single spacing token, speeding up page composition.

**Stage 3 — Pattern:**
- *Definition:* Structured, often two-dimensional tiling of a repeat unit.
- *Design example:* **Islamic geometric tile work** (e.g., the Alhambra in Spain).
- *Engineering / production benefit:* **Modular fabrication** — a single tile mold can produce thousands of units, cutting manufacturing cost and time.

**Conclusion:** The continuum reflects a *progression of structural complexity* — from a single intentional copy, to a measured cadence, to a fully tessellated system.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $N_c = 12$ total colored elements.
- $n_c = 9$ brand-colored elements.
- Poster dimensions: $600\ \text{mm} \times 400\ \text{mm}$.
- Motif size: $20\ \text{mm} \times 20\ \text{mm}$.

**Step (i) — Color Repetition Index (CRI):**

By definition:

$$
CRI = \dfrac{n_c}{N_c} = \dfrac{9}{12} = 0.75
$$

**Interpretation:** A CRI of $0.75$ (or **75 %**) indicates **strong chromatic unity** — three out of every four colored elements are on-brand, which a KTU examiner will mark as a *cohesive palette*.

**Step (ii) — Tessellation Coverage (P<sub>cov</sub>):**

The motif area:

$$
A_{motif} = 20 \times 20 = 400\ \text{mm}^2
$$

The poster area:

$$
A_{poster} = 600 \times 400 = 240{,}000\ \text{mm}^2
$$

Tessellation coverage:

$$
P_{cov} = \dfrac{A_{motif}}{A_{poster}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{400}{240{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
P_{cov} = \dfrac{1}{600} \times 100\% \approx 0.1667\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Each individual motif occupies only **≈ 0.17 %** of the poster. This is realistic — the *coverage per tile* is tiny, but the *cumulative* coverage of all tiles together will be **100 %** because they tile edge-to-edge.

**Step (iii) — Total number of motif tiles:**

Number of tiles along the width:

$$
N_w = \dfrac{600}{20} = 30\ \text{tiles}
$$

Number of tiles along the height:

$$
N_h = \dfrac{400}{20} = 20\ \text{tiles}
$$

Total number of tiles:

$$
N_{total} = N_w \times N_h = 30 \times 20 = 600\ \text{tiles}
$$

**Verification via area:**

$$
N_{total} = \dfrac{A_{poster}}{A_{motif}} = \dfrac{240{,}000}{400} = 600\ \text{tiles} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| (i) Color Repetition Index | $CRI = 0.75$ (75 %) — strong unity |
| (ii) Tessellation Coverage per tile | $P_{cov} \approx 0.167\%$ |
| (iii) Total motif tiles | $N_{total} = 600$ |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes — where marks are routinely lost:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Repetition with Pattern (–2 marks):** Many students use the two terms interchangeably. Repetition is the *act*; Pattern is the *structured result*. Examiners will deduct marks if you do not distinguish them.
>
> 2. **Omitting the formula derivation (–2 to –3 marks):** For 7-mark numerical sub-parts, simply writing the *final answer* is **not enough**. KTU examiners award marks for (a) writing the governing equation, (b) showing substitution, (c) isolating the variable, and (d) interpreting the result. Always show all four.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the 8-px / baseline grid comment (–1 mark):** Part (b) of Question B-A explicitly asks you to comment on baseline-grid compliance. A purely numerical answer without the design-system comment is treated as incomplete.
>
> 4. **Writing "rhythm = pattern" (–2 marks):** In the Part B diagram question, students often draw only two boxes (Repetition and Pattern) and skip Rhythm. Rhythm is the *middle concept* and carries dedicated marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units (–1 mark):** Always carry units ($px$, $mm$, $pt$) in numerical answers. A bare number without units is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Repetition (Module 2)**

- **Definition (must-memorize):** Repetition = intentional duplication of a visual element to create **unity, rhythm, and brand consistency**.
- **Three-stage continuum:** Repetition → Rhythm → Pattern (one-act → one-D-cadenced → two-D-tessellated).
- **Key formula:** $L = N \cdot w + (N - 1) \cdot RI$ — memorize the master layout equation.
- **Beat Frequency:** $f = \dfrac{1}{RI}$ — inversely proportional to the interval.
- **Color Repetition Index:** $CRI = \dfrac{n_c}{N_c} \in [0, 1]$ — closer to 1 means stronger chromatic unity.
- **Tessellation Coverage (per tile):** $P_{cov} = \dfrac{A_{motif}}{A_{total}} \times 100\%$.
- **Total tile count:** $N_{total} = \dfrac{A_{total}}{A_{motif}} = N_w \times N_h$.
- **Modular scale:** $s_i = s_0 \cdot r^i$ — common ratios are $1.2$ (Minor Third), $1.25$ (Major Third), $1.333$ (Perfect Fourth), $1.5$ (Perfect Fifth), and $\phi \approx 1.618$ (Golden Ratio).
- **Baseline grid:** Always anchor intervals to multiples of the grid unit $G$ (typically 8 px in digital, 12 pt in print).
- **Real-world anchors to mention in any answer:** *Nike Swoosh*, *Coca-Cola red*, *Apple logotype*, *Material Design 8-px grid*, *Alhambra tile work*, *newspaper columns*.
- **Engineering / production benefits to mention:** (1) Single-source asset reuse, (2) Predictable layout math, (3) Modular fabrication savings, (4) Brand equity growth, (5) Reduced cognitive load on the viewer.
- **Examiner's red flags to avoid:** (a) Using "rhythm" and "pattern" interchangeably, (b) Skipping derivation steps, (c) Dropping units, (d) Ignoring baseline-grid discussion when prompted.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Line and Shape
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT529 - VISUAL DESIGN AND COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Line and Shape

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of a Line

In the formal vocabulary of **Visual Design and Communication**, a *line* is the most fundamental and versatile element of design. It is technically defined as a **one-dimensional visual mark** that connects two or more points, possessing length as its primary attribute and effectively negligible width. A line can be real (physically drawn), implied (suggested by the alignment of elements), or psychological (perceived by the viewer's mind between separate elements).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A line is the path of a moving point through space. It is the building block of all visual communication, defining edges, forms, and boundaries.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a line like the **trail left by a pen** moving across a blank page. If you take a single sheet of white paper and drag a pencil from one corner to the opposite corner, the dark graphite trace that appears is, in its purest form, a line. In real life, lines are everywhere: the horizon where the sky meets the ocean, the straight edge of a ruler, the curving path of a river seen from an airplane, or the contour of a mountain range. The brain instantly recognizes these as lines because they mark a transition or a direction in space.

### 1.2 Definition of a Shape

A *shape* is a **two-dimensional enclosed area** that is defined by boundaries created through lines, color, value, or texture. A shape has both length and width, and its contour defines its identity. Shapes are categorized primarily into geometric (regular, mathematical) and organic (free-flowing, natural) forms, along with the concept of **positive and negative space**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Design Principle:**
> Every shape is technically constructed by lines that close back upon themselves to enclose an area. A line that does not enclose a region remains a line; the moment it closes into a loop, it becomes a shape. This is the foundational transition from **one-dimensional to two-dimensional** visual design.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine cutting a piece of colored paper. The outline you cut along is the *line*; the piece of paper you now hold in your hand is the *shape*. The cutout space it left behind in the original sheet is its *negative space counterpart*. A circular cookie cutter pressed into dough creates a shape (the cookie) bounded by a line (the cutter's edge), surrounded by the negative space (the remaining dough).

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

- **Length**: The measurable extent of a line from one endpoint to another (measured in **millimeters, centimeters, or pixels** in digital design).
- **Width / Thickness (Weight)**: The visual heaviness of a line, often called its **stroke weight**.
- **Direction**: The orientation of a line in space (horizontal, vertical, diagonal, or curved).
- **Closed vs. Open Shape**: A *closed shape* is fully enclosed by lines; an *open shape* has unconnected boundaries.
- **Positive Space**: The actual subject or object occupying the design area.
- **Negative Space**: The empty area surrounding and defining the positive space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for Digital Line Weight:** In professional vector design software (Adobe Illustrator, Figma, CorelDRAW), line weight is measured in **points (pt)**, where 1 pt = 0.3528 mm. Web standard is **1 px to 2 px** for UI elements.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Lines

Lines are classified based on their **direction, character, and function** within a composition. Understanding these classifications is critical for KTU 2024 evaluation, as examiners frequently test the application of specific line types in design problems.

#### 2.1.1 Based on Direction

1. **Horizontal Line**
   - Runs parallel to the horizon.
   - Conveys: Stability, calm, rest, tranquility, vastness.
   - Use case: Landscape compositions, minimalist layouts, architectural drawings.

2. **Vertical Line**
   - Runs perpendicular to the horizon.
   - Conveys: Strength, formality, dignity, rigidity, height.
   - Use case: Corporate logos, fashion design, structural illustrations.

3. **Diagonal Line**
   - Slants at an angle between horizontal and vertical.
   - Conveys: Movement, action, dynamism, tension, instability.
   - Use case: Sports graphics, action scenes, dynamic posters.

4. **Curved Line**
   - Bends smoothly without sharp angles.
   - Conveys: Grace, fluidity, softness, organic flow.
   - Subtypes: **Spiral** (rotational growth), **Wave** (rhythmic undulation), **S-Curve** (sinuous balance).

#### 2.1.2 Based on Character

| Type | Visual Appearance | Psychological Effect | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Straight Line** | Rigid, unbending | Order, control, logic | Technical drawings, architecture |
| **Curved Line** | Smooth, flowing | Harmony, elegance | Fashion, organic branding |
| **Thick Line** | Heavy visual weight | Boldness, importance | Emphasis, headlines |
| **Thin Line** | Light, delicate | Subtlety, precision | Detailing, technical illustrations |
| **Dotted Line** | Interrupted marks | Implied connection, hidden elements | Hidden objects, dotted paths |
| **Dashed Line** | Equal short segments | Movement, temporary nature | Cutting lines, boundaries |
| **Wavy Line** | Continuous undulation | Energy, water, vibration | Nature illustrations, sound waves |
| **Zigzag Line** | Sharp angular changes | Excitement, danger, instability | Lightning, electricity, alerts |
| **Implied Line** | Not drawn, suggested | Viewer completes the form | Gestalt grouping in composition |
| **Psychic Line** | Invisible, conceptual | Direction of gaze, motion path | Photography, leading the eye |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **Implied Line** and **Psychic Line** are favorite exam topics because they test a student's understanding of **Gestalt Principles of Perception** rather than mere drawing skill.

#### 2.1.3 Based on Function in Composition

- **Outline / Contour Line**: Defines the outer boundary of a form.
- **Construction Line**: A guide used during the drafting process.
- **Center Line**: A symmetrical axis line in technical drawings.
- **Dimension Line**: Indicates measurements with arrowheads.
- **Leader Line**: Connects a label or annotation to a feature.
- **Hatching Line**: Repeated parallel lines used to indicate shading or texture.
- **Hidden Line**: Dashed lines representing obscured edges in technical drawings.

### 2.2 Classification of Shapes

#### 2.2.1 Geometric Shapes

Geometric shapes are **mathematically precise, regular, and constructed using rules of geometry**. They are the foundation of all constructed environments.

- **Circle**: Perfectly round, no beginning or end. Symbolizes unity, wholeness, infinity.
- **Square**: Four equal sides, four right angles. Symbolizes stability, order, equality.
- **Triangle**: Three sides, conveys direction and energy. Subtypes: equilateral, isosceles, scalene, right-angled.
- **Rectangle**: Four sides with opposite sides equal. Symbolizes balance, structure.
- **Polygon**: Multi-sided closed figure (pentagon, hexagon, octagon).
- **Oval / Ellipse**: Stretched circle. Symbolizes movement, sophistication.
- **Trapezoid, Rhombus, Parallelogram**: Quadrilaterals with distinct properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Property:** A shape with **n** straight sides has the sum of its interior angles equal to:
>
> $$\text{Sum of Interior Angles} = (n - 2) \times 180^\circ$$
>
> where **n** is the number of sides. This formula is highly useful when verifying geometric constructions in design problems.

#### 2.2.2 Organic (Biomorphic) Shapes

Organic shapes are **irregular, free-form, and typically inspired by nature**. They have no mathematical predictability and are defined by flowing, asymmetric contours.

- Examples: leaves, clouds, pebbles, splashes of water, animal silhouettes.
- Convey: Naturalness, comfort, spontaneity, warmth, humanity.

#### 2.2.3 Abstract Shapes

- Shapes that are **simplified or stylized** representations of real objects, reduced to their essential visual characteristics.
- Example: A pictogram of a person walking is an abstract shape.

#### 2.2.4 Positive and Negative Shapes

The interaction between an object (positive shape) and the empty space around or within it (negative shape) is a critical design consideration.

- **Positive Space**: The area occupied by the subject.
- **Negative Space**: The empty or background area, which can itself form recognizable shapes (e.g., the famous FedEx logo where negative space forms an arrow).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** When negative space forms a meaningful shape, it is called a **Figurative Negative** or **Double Image**. This is a classic board exam question and demonstrates advanced design thinking.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sum of interior angles of a polygon | $(n - 2) \times 180^\circ$ | Degrees ($^\circ$) | Polygon design validation |
| Area of a circle | $A = \pi r^2$ | Square units ($m^2$, $cm^2$, $px^2$) | Circular shape construction |
| Circumference of a circle | $C = 2\pi r$ | Units of length | Perimeter of circular shapes |
| Area of a rectangle | $A = l \times w$ | Square units | Rectangular shape construction |
| Area of a triangle | $A = \frac{1}{2} \times b \times h$ | Square units | Triangular composition |
| Pythagorean relation | $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ | Square of length units | Right-angle shape construction |
| Golden ratio (shape proportion) | $\varphi = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ | Dimensionless | Aesthetic shape proportions |
| Rule of thirds (composition) | $\frac{W}{3}$ and $\frac{2W}{3}$ axes | Length units | Placement of key shapes |
| Stroke-to-fill ratio (typography) | $\text{Stroke Weight} : \text{Cap Height} = 1:8$ to $1:12$ | Dimensionless | Line weight in design |
| Point-to-pixel conversion | $1 \text{ pt} = \frac{1}{72} \text{ inch} \approx 0.3528 \text{ mm}$ | Length units | Digital line specification |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipe symbol `|` is used in any cell above to preserve markdown table integrity.** The symbol $\vert$ in LaTeX is reserved for mathematical absolute value operations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering and Design Utility

- **Architecture and Civil Engineering**: Lines define structural plans; geometric shapes determine load-bearing geometry.
- **Mechanical Engineering and CAD**: Orthographic projections rely on visible lines, hidden lines, and center lines.
- **User Interface (UI) Design**: Stroke weights define icon clarity, alignment lines create visual hierarchy.
- **Animation and Motion Graphics**: The 12 Principles of Animation (Disney) begin with **"Squash and Stretch"** rooted in shape morphing, and **"Follow Through"** rooted in line trajectory.
- **Industrial Design**: Product silhouettes are constructed from continuous lines that close into functional shapes.
- **Fashion Design**: Garment construction uses dart lines, seam lines, and grain lines, all of which define the final shape of the apparel.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Line to Shape — The Closure Principle

A line is one-dimensional; a shape is two-dimensional. The transformation from one to the other requires the line to **close upon itself to enclose a finite area**. This analytical proof establishes the geometric and topological basis for this transition.

**Step 1: Define the One-Dimensional Line**

A line segment in 2D space is defined by two endpoints, $P_1$ and $P_2$, with coordinates:

$$P_1 = (x_1, y_1), \quad P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$$

The length of this open line segment is given by the Euclidean distance formula:

$$L = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$

At this stage, the line is **open** and encloses no area. The enclosed area $A = 0$.

**Step 2: Introduce a Third Point to Initiate a Polygon**

Add a third point $P_3 = (x_3, y_3)$ and connect $P_2$ to $P_3$ with another line segment. We now have two line segments forming an open chain. Still, the area enclosed is zero because the chain has no closure.

**Step 3: Closure of the Path**

To form a closed shape, we must connect the final point back to the initial point. For an $n$-sided polygon, this requires $n$ line segments, with the last segment closing the path:

$$P_n \to P_1$$

Once closed, the path encloses a non-zero area.

**Step 4: Calculate the Enclosed Area (Shoelace Formula)**

For a polygon with vertices listed in order, the enclosed area is:

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i \cdot y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} \cdot y_i) \right\vert$$

where the indices wrap around such that $P_{n+1} = P_1$. The vertical bars denote absolute value to ensure a positive area result.

**Step 5: Apply to a Specific Case — A Square**

Let the four vertices of a square of side length $s$ be:

$$P_1 = (0, 0), \quad P_2 = (s, 0), \quad P_3 = (s, s), \quad P_4 = (0, s)$$

Substituting into the formula:

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert (0 \cdot 0 - s \cdot 0) + (s \cdot s - s \cdot 0) + (s \cdot s - 0 \cdot s) + (0 \cdot 0 - 0 \cdot s) \right\vert$$

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert 0 + s^2 + s^2 + 0 \right\vert = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2s^2 = s^2$$

This confirms the standard result:

$$A_{\text{square}} = s^2$$

**Step 6: Analytical Conclusion**

> A shape exists **if and only if** its boundary path is closed and encloses a non-zero area. The line is the *generator*; the shape is the *enclosed result*. Every shape in design can be decomposed into its bounding line segments and verified using the Shoelace formula.

### 3.2 Derivation: The Golden Ratio in Shape Proportions

The Golden Ratio ($\varphi$) governs many of the most aesthetically pleasing shapes and compositions.

**Step 1: Define the Ratio**

A line segment is divided into two parts, $a$ and $b$ (with $a > b$), such that:

$$\frac{a + b}{a} = \frac{a}{b} = \varphi$$

**Step 2: Solve for $\varphi$**

Let $a = 1$ and $b = x$. Then:

$$\frac{1 + x}{1} = \frac{1}{x}$$

$$1 + x = \frac{1}{x}$$

Multiply both sides by $x$:

$$x + x^2 = 1$$

Rearrange into standard quadratic form:

$$x^2 + x - 1 = 0$$

**Step 3: Apply the Quadratic Formula**

For a quadratic $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$, the roots are:

$$x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$$

Substituting $a = 1$, $b = 1$, $c = -1$:

$$x = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1^2 - 4(1)(-1)}}{2(1)} = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 4}}{2} = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}$$

Taking the positive root (since length must be positive):

$$x = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 0.618$$

Therefore:

$$\varphi = \frac{1}{x} = \frac{1}{0.618} \approx 1.618$$

Or directly:

$$\varphi = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618033988\ldots$$

**Step 4: Application to Shape Design**

A rectangle with the Golden Ratio of sides is called a **Golden Rectangle**, considered the most aesthetically pleasing rectangular shape:

$$\frac{L}{W} = \varphi \approx 1.618$$

This ratio governs credit card dimensions, classical architectural façades, book proportions, and logo design grids.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Line Type

| Design Goal | Recommended Line Type | Psychological Effect | Common Mistake to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Convey stability in architecture | Horizontal + Vertical | Grounded, formal | Avoid diagonals (suggest instability) |
| Create energy in advertising | Diagonal, Zigzag | Action, urgency | Do not overuse; causes visual fatigue |
| Communicate organic brand | Curved, Wavy | Friendly, soft | Avoid sharp corners in organic contexts |
| Show technical precision | Thin, even, Straight | Accuracy, professionalism | Avoid hand-drawn or wavy strokes |
| Lead the viewer's eye | Implied / Psychic | Directs attention | Do not overuse; hierarchy gets lost |
| Highlight emphasis | Thick, Bold Line | Importance, weight | Do not thicken every line equally |
| Suggest hidden elements | Dashed / Dotted | Concealment, secondary | Reserve for technical/hidden meaning only |
| Create balance and flow | S-Curve | Elegant rhythm | Avoid S-curves that distort symmetry |

### 3.4 Line-to-Shape Construction Sequence (Algorithmic Logic for Designers)

The following logical sequence describes how a designer systematically transforms lines into shape compositions:

1. **Identify the Message**: Determine what concept the shape must communicate.
2. **Choose Shape Category**: Select geometric (rational/order) or organic (natural/flowing).
3. **Establish the Grid**: Set up a base grid or use the Rule of Thirds.
4. **Sketch Construction Lines**: Light preliminary lines to define proportions.
5. **Refine Contour Lines**: Convert construction lines into finalized shape boundaries.
6. **Apply Closure**: Ensure all bounding lines connect to enclose a region.
7. **Evaluate Positive/Negative Space**: Confirm that the void around the shape is intentional and balanced.
8. **Verify Proportions**: Use Golden Ratio or Rule of Thirds as a sanity check.
9. **Add Detail Lines**: Hatching, cross-hatching, or contour lines for shading and depth.
10. **Final Review**: Check if the composition reads correctly at multiple viewing distances.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Examiners reward students who demonstrate the *process* of going from line to shape, not just the *result*. Always articulate each step in your answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Line Classification System
