# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Derivation Matrix

## 3.1 The Canonical Six-Step Process for Creating a Simplified Drawing

This is the **exam-ready procedural answer** that KTU examiners expect in Part B questions on this topic. Memorize it; rehearse it; reproduce it with discipline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Six-Step Simplified Drawing Pipeline:**
> **Observe → Analyze → Decompose → Silhouette → Internalize → Refine**

### Step 1 — Observation (The Reference Gathering Phase)

**Purpose:** Build a complete mental + visual database of the subject before any simplification decision is made.

**Operational Actions:**
- Study the object from at least **3 viewpoints** (front, side, top — or three-quarter if pictorial).
- Note: proportions, dominant material cues, function-defining features.
- Capture gesture, motion, and pose if the subject is animate.

**Transition Logic:** Observation ends when you can describe the object *without looking at it*. The visual reference is now internalized.

### Step 2 — Analysis (Feature Classification Phase)

**Purpose:** Classify every visible feature into one of three categories.

$$\text{Feature}(f) \in \{\text{Critical}, \text{Supportive}, \text{Discardable}\}$$

**Classification Rules:**

| Category | Rule of Thumb | Example (Car) |
|---|---|---|
| **Critical** | Removing it makes the object unidentifiable | Wheels, body silhouette, windshield |
| **Supportive** | Adds character/scale but not identity | Door lines, headlights, grille |
| **Discardable** | Decorative, ultra-fine, or photorealistic | Reflections, micro-screws, surface noise |

**Transition Logic:** Approximately **15–25%** of features are Critical, **30–40%** are Supportive, and the rest are Discardable.

### Step 3 — Decomposition (Primitive Mapping Phase)

**Purpose:** Reduce the object into a small set of geometric primitives.

For each part of the object, ask: *"What primitive volume is this closest to?"*

$$\text{Part}(p) \mapsto V_i \in \{\text{Cube, Sphere, Cylinder, Cone, Torus, Pyramid}\}$$

**Worked Example — A Simple Wooden Chair:**

- Seat → **Cuboid**
- Backrest → **Cuboid / Flat plane**
- Four legs → **Cylinders** (or cuboids for a more geometric/stylized look)
- Optional stretchers → **Cylinders**

**Transition Logic:** Decomposition succeeds when the primitives, *if rendered correctly*, would already suggest the chair.

### Step 4 — Silhouette Construction (Outline Anchoring Phase)

**Purpose:** Establish the **single, closed, continuous outer contour** that defines the object.

**Operational Actions:**
1. Draw the silhouette in **one confident stroke** (or with minimal lifts).
2. Apply **uniform line weight** (e.g., 3 mm marker width).
3. Verify with the **silhouette test** — fill the outline solid black; the form must still read.

**Quality Check:**

$$\text{Silhouette Score} = \frac{\text{Recognizable Identity from Black Silhouette}}{1.0}$$

A score above **0.7** is acceptable for KTU submissions.

**Transition Logic:** Once the silhouette passes the test, the *identity contract* with the viewer is sealed. Internal lines only refine — they do not redefine.

### Step 5 — Internalization (Selective Feature Insertion)

**Purpose:** Add back the Critical and selected Supportive features using the **4-Question Test**.

For each retained feature:
1. Place it in correct proportion to the silhouette.
2. Use **lighter line weight** (~50–60% of silhouette weight) to establish hierarchy.
3. Avoid crossing or breaking the silhouette line.

**Line Weight Hierarchy:**

$$W_{\text{silhouette}} > W_{\text{primary internal}} > W_{\text{secondary internal}} > W_{\text{texture}}$$

**Typical Ratio:** $4 : 3 : 2 : 1$ mm.

**Transition Logic:** Internalization ends when adding one more line would *reduce* clarity rather than increase it.

### Step 6 — Refinement (Polish & Style Codification)

**Purpose:** Apply finishing touches — style consistency, negative space balance, optional tone/color, and final proportional correction.

**Operational Actions:**
- Erase construction/guideline marks.
- Verify uniform stroke weight across the drawing.
- Check **negative space balance**: 40–60% empty area.
- Apply a single stylistic filter consistently (flat / isometric / line-only).

**Final Quality Formula (recall from Section 2):**

$$Q(S) = 0.45 \cdot I + 0.45 \cdot L - 0.10 \cdot \frac{S}{C}$$

A high-quality KTU submission achieves **$Q(S) \geq 0.75$**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Simplifying a Table Lamp

To make this concrete, let us walk through the full simplification of a **table lamp** — a common KTU Module 3 exam subject.

**Original Reference:** A realistic photo of a ceramic table lamp with a fabric shade, decorative bulb base, pull cord, and visible shadow.

### Phase A — Feature Classification

| Feature | Category | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| Fabric shade outline | Critical | **Keep** |
| Bulb base silhouette | Critical | **Keep** |
| Pull cord | Discardable | **Delete** |
| Surface shadow gradient | Discardable | **Delete** |
| Shade seam line | Supportive | **Keep (light)** |
| Base decorative ring | Supportive | **Keep (light)** |
| Realistic ceramic shine | Discardable | **Delete** |

### Phase B — Primitive Decomposition

- **Lamp shade** → **Truncated cone (frustum)**
- **Bulb base** → **Cylinder + smaller cylinder stem**
- **Cord** → **Deleted entirely** (decorative discard)
- **Top finial** → **Small sphere**

### Phase C — Silhouette Drawing

A continuous closed contour combining: **frustum top + frustum bottom + cylinder body + stem + sphere finial**. One stroke where possible.

### Phase D — Internalization

Add:
- Shade seam (light line, horizontal arc).
- Base decorative ring (one thin ellipse).
- Optional bulb outline (very faint, only if it serves scale).

### Phase E — Refinement

Apply **flat pictorial style**: solid black silhouette, no shading, balanced negative space, centered composition.

### Final Evaluation

- $I$ (identity retention) ≈ 0.90 — the lamp is unmistakable.
- $L$ (legibility) ≈ 0.95 — clean and unambiguous.
- $S/C$ (line economy) ≈ 0.25 — only 25% of the original detail preserved.

$$Q(S) = 0.45(0.90) + 0.45(0.95) - 0.10(0.25) = 0.405 + 0.4275 - 0.025 = 0.8075$$

**Verdict:** $Q(S) = 0.8075 \geq 0.75$ → **Excellent KTU-quality simplified drawing.**

## 3.3 Decision Matrix — When to Keep, Lighten, or Delete a Line

| Situation | Decision | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Feature is on the silhouette boundary | **Keep at full weight** | Defines identity |
| Feature is inside the form, marks a plane change | **Keep at medium weight** | Adds structural reading |
| Feature is decorative texture (wood grain, fabric weave) | **Delete** | Increases noise, lowers legibility |
| Feature is a shadow / highlight gradient | **Delete or replace with flat tone** | Simplification rule |
| Feature repeats rhythmically (vents, slats) | **Suggest 2–3, then imply rest** | Economy of marks |
| Feature is realistic material cue (reflection) | **Delete** | Belongs to realism, not simplification |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When the question asks "How did you decide what to keep?" — answer with **the 4-Question Test** by name. Examiners reward explicit methodology.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Simplified Drawing Pipeline (Mermaid Workflow)

The following diagram maps the **full procedural pipeline** for creating a simplified drawing, with decision gates and feedback loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Subject Selected] --> B[Step 1: Observe]
    B --> B1[Study from 3+ viewpoints]
    B1 --> B2[Note proportions, features, motion]
    B2 --> C[Step 2: Analyze Features]
    C --> C1{Feature Classification}
    C1 -->|Critical| C2[Keep Mandatory]
    C1 -->|Supportive| C3[Apply 4-Question Test]
    C1 -->|Discardable| C4[Delete]
    C3 -->|Yes to any| C2
    C3 -->|All No| C4
    C2 --> D[Step 3: Decompose into Primitives]
    D --> D1[Map each part to Cube Sphere Cylinder Cone Torus Pyramid]
    D1 --> E[Step 4: Construct Silhouette]
    E --> E1[Draw single closed contour in one stroke]
    E1 --> E2{Silhouette Test Pass?}
    E2 -->|No| E3[Re-draw contour]
    E3 --> E1
    E2 -->|Yes| F[Step 5: Internalize Selected Features]
    F --> F1[Apply line weight hierarchy 4 to 3 to 2 to 1 mm]
    F1 --> G[Step 6: Refine and Style]
    G --> G1[Erase construction lines]
    G --> G2[Check negative space 40 to 60 percent]
    G --> G3[Apply consistent stylistic filter]
    G3 --> H{Quality Score Q of S greater or equal 0.75}
    H -->|No| H1[Return to Step 5 or Step 4]
    H1 --> F
    H -->|Yes| I[End: KTU-Quality Simplified Drawing]
```

## 4.2 The Four-Pillar Conceptual Architecture

This diagram shows the **four pillars** that every simplified drawing must satisfy, and how they interlock to produce the final output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Pillar 1: Structural Decomposition]
        P1A[Form Analysis] --> P1B[Primitive Identification]
        P1B --> P1C[Gesture Bounding Box]
    end

    subgraph P2[Pillar 2: Silhouette Anchoring]
        P2A[Single Closed Contour] --> P2B[Uniform Line Weight]
        P2B --> P2C[Silhouette Black Test]
    end

    subgraph P3[Pillar 3: Selective Internalization]
        P3A[Critical Features] --> P3B[Supportive Features]
        P3B --> P3C[Discardable Deleted]
    end

    subgraph P4[Pillar 4: Stylistic Codification]
        P4A[Style Selection] --> P4B[Consistency Application]
        P4B --> P4C[Intent Audience Match]
    end

    P1C --> Q[Simplified Drawing Output]
    P2C --> Q
    P3C --> Q
    P4C --> Q

    Q --> R[Communication Clarity]
    Q --> S[Identity Preservation]
    Q --> T[Visual Economy]
```

## 4.3 Feature Classification Decision Tree

This tree visualizes the **decision logic** for any single feature encountered during the Analyze step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F[Feature Encountered] --> Q1{Removing it makes object unrecognizable?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| K1[CRITICAL: Keep at full weight]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Does it mark a plane or volume change?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Does it add scale or character?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| K2[SUPPORTIVE: Keep at lighter weight]
    Q3 -->|No| K3[SUPPORTIVE: Consider keep or delete]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{Is it texture, shadow, or reflection?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| D1[DISCARDABLE: Delete]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Is it rhythmically repeated detail?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| D2[DISCARDABLE: Suggest 2 to 3 then imply]
    Q5 -->|No| D3[DISCARDABLE: Delete if not essential]

    K1 --> OUT[Feature Decision Logged]
    K2 --> OUT
    K3 --> OUT
    D1 --> OUT
    D2 --> OUT
    D3 --> OUT
```

## 4.4 The Quality Function — Geometric Visualization

The following block diagram shows how the three components of $Q(S)$ combine to yield the final simplification quality score.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Inputs[Inputs to Quality Function]
        I[Identity Retention Score I] -->|weight alpha equals 0.45| M1
        L[Legibility Score L] -->|weight beta equals 0.45| M2
        R[Line Ratio S over C] -->|penalty gamma equals 0.10| M3
    end

    M1[Alpha times I] --> Sum[Sum Operation]
    M2[Beta times L] --> Sum
    M3[Gamma times R] --> Sub[Subtract from Sum]
    Sum --> Sub
    Sub --> Q[Q of S Output]

    Q --> Eval{Q of S greater or equal 0.75}
    Eval -->|Yes| PASS[Accept: KTU Quality Met]
    Eval -->|No| FAIL[Iterate: Return to Pipeline Step 4 or 5]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams in Exam Answers:** KTU examiners award marks for *visual organization* of answers. A Mermaid-style or hand-drawn flowchart accompanying a Part B answer significantly boosts the structure marks. Even a rough sketch-flow on the answer sheet demonstrates process thinking.
